### `-7013890438520559398`

### `6962706727076805207`
> A **bait car** (also called a decoy car, hot car, or trap car) is used to catch car thieves. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> These vehicles are modified with audio/video surveillance technology and can be remotely monitored and controlled. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> Bait cars set up to catch car thieves may include GPS tracking and a "kill switch" that allows police to remotely disable the engine and lock all doors to prevent escape. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> The bait car is typically filled with valuable items and parked in a high auto-theft area, often left unlocked with keys in the ignition. [alce::6962706727076805207::p1]

### `1029755223764707835`
> The books of the New Testament were all or nearly all written by Jewish Christians—that is, Jewish disciples of Jesus, during the first and early second centuries. [alce::1029755223764707835::p10]
> Luke wrote the Gospel of Luke and the Book of Acts, though scholars are divided as to whether Luke was a Gentile or a Hellenistic Jew. [alce::1029755223764707835::p10]
> Church tradition has held that John the Apostle is the author of the Gospel of John and four other books of the New Testament—the three Epistles of John and the Book of Revelation. [alce::1029755223764707835::p55]
> The Gospels were written between 68-110 CE, Acts between 95-110, Epistles between 51-110 CE and Revelation in c. 95 CE. [alce::1029755223764707835::p10]

Cited: alce::1029755223764707835::p10, alce::1029755223764707835::p55

### `-6253059790504461986`
> Three key platforms are used to test for lipids: mass spectrometry, chromatography, and nuclear magnetic resonance. [alce::-6253059790504461986::p1]
> More specifically, thin layer chromatography (TLC) is the simplest method of lipid separation and offers a rapid and comprehensive screening tool. [alce::-6253059790504461986::p2]
> For blood-based testing, a lipid profile or "lipid panel" is a panel of blood tests that serves as an initial screening tool for abnormalities in lipids, such as cholesterol and triglycerides. [alce::-6253059790504461986::p7]
> Additional analytical techniques include X-ray diffraction, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), nuclear magnetic resonance (including HNMR and PNMR), fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP), and solid-phase extraction (SPE) chromatography. [alce::-6253059790504461986::p14]

Cited: alce::-6253059790504461986::p1, alce::-6253059790504461986::p14, alce::-6253059790504461986::p2, alce::-6253059790504461986::p7

### `4965896127144803833`
> The downfall of the Inca Empire resulted from multiple interconnected factors:

The Spanish conquest was the proximate cause of the empire's collapse. [alce::4965896127144803833::p4]
> During the Spanish conquest, Francisco Pizarro captured the Inca emperor Atahualpa and used him to control the empire, and the Spanish eventually executed Atahualpa, effectively ending the empire. [alce::4965896127144803833::p85]
> However, internal instability also contributed significantly. Smallpox, introduced from Panama and preceding the Spanish conquerors, swept through the Inca Empire between 1524 and 1526, killing the Incan ruler Huayna Capac and most of his family including his heir, which caused the fall of the Incan political structure and contributed to a civil war between the brothers Atahualpa and Huáscar. [alce::4965896127144803833::p52]
> Pizarro took advantage of this internal conflict to carry out his conquest. [alce::4965896127144803833::p52]
> Some scholars suggest the empire may have already been in decline before the Spanish arrived. [alce::4965896127144803833::p4]
> Following the conquest, the indigenous population dramatically collapsed due to exploitation, socioeconomic change, and epidemic diseases introduced by the Spanish. [alce::4965896127144803833::p2]

Cited: alce::4965896127144803833::p2, alce::4965896127144803833::p4, alce::4965896127144803833::p52, alce::4965896127144803833::p85

### `-5001543574321430501`
> As blues moved into Chicago's South Side, an electric blues style developed. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p1]
> This electric style had been developed earlier in Electric Memphis and Texas Blues, but in Chicago it became harder and edgier, with instruments like the saxophone incorporated into the music, giving it a sometimes jazzy feel. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p35]
> Chicago blues is characterized as an electric blues style of urban blues that evolved from classic blues following the Great Migration of African Americans from the southern United States to industrial cities of the north. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p1]

### `3189494571253889631`
> The solid part of Earth is called the geosphere. [alce::3189494571253889631::p4]
> In modern Earth system science, the geosphere refers to the solid parts of the Earth and is used along with the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and biosphere to describe the Earth's systems. [alce::3189494571253889631::p4]
> The term lithosphere is sometimes used instead of geosphere or solid Earth, and it refers to the uppermost layers of the solid Earth, including oceanic and continental crustal rocks and the uppermost mantle. [alce::3189494571253889631::p6]

### `-1528483370616468286`
> According to tradition, Moses was the author of the first five books of the Bible. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p0]
> However, the books themselves do not name any author, as authorship was not considered important by the society that produced them. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p2]
> Modern scholarship questions this traditional attribution, with philosophers and theologians such as Thomas Hobbes, Benedict Spinoza, and Richard Simon challenging Mosaic authorship based on various inconsistencies and anomalies in the texts. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p0]
> The tradition of Mosaic authorship likely began with the law-code of Deuteronomy and was then gradually extended to the other books. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p41]

### `6896427128230648690`
> The Battle of Philippi refers to two different historical events, so I need to clarify which one you're asking about:

**Roman Civil War (42 BC):**
Mark Antony and Octavian of the Second Triumvirate won the Battle of Philippi. [alce::6896427128230648690::p69]
> The battles of Philippi ended the civil war in favor of the Caesarian faction, with the defeat of the Liberators (Marcus Junius Brutus and Gaius Cassius Longinus). [alce::6896427128230648690::p0]
> **American Civil War (1861):**
The Union won the Battle of Philippi. [alce::6896427128230648690::p1]
> It was fought on June 3, 1861, in Philippi, Virginia (now West Virginia), and was the first organized land action of the American Civil War. [alce::6896427128230648690::p1]

### `1719387094791600068`
> The muscle fibers under the control of a single motor axon are the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals. [alce::1719387094791600068::p6]
> A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals. [alce::1719387094791600068::p6]
> One motor neuron may innervate several muscle fibers. [alce::1719387094791600068::p34]
> All muscle fibers in a motor unit are of the same fiber type, and when a motor unit is activated, all of its fibers contract. [alce::1719387094791600068::p6]

Cited: alce::1719387094791600068::p34, alce::1719387094791600068::p6

### `8216960630679687356`
> John L. O'Sullivan, a New York newspaper editor, is generally credited with coining the phrase "Manifest Destiny" in July 1845. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]
> Manifest Destiny meant the "design of Providence" supporting the territorial expansion of the United States. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]
> The phrase captured the idea that the young American nation was destined to rule all of the North American continent. [alce::8216960630679687356::p77]
> Manifest Destiny was a justification for expansion and westward movement, or an ideology or doctrine that helped to promote the progress of civilization. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]

### `-9150237805953541495`
> Emory S. Bogardus tried to evaluate attitude through the social distance. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p6]
> He created the Bogardus social distance scale as a psychological testing scale to empirically measure people's willingness to participate in social contacts of varying degrees of closeness with members of diverse social groups. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p1]
> For Bogardus, social distance is a function of affective distance between the members of two groups, essentially measuring how much or little sympathy the members of a group feel for another group. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p1]

Cited: alce::-9150237805953541495::p1, alce::-9150237805953541495::p6

### `-2777825999784077693`
> Karl Landsteiner figured out a way to determine the type of blood. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p1]
> In 1900, he found that blood sera from different persons would clump together when mixed, and in 1901 he identified the three blood groups A, B, and O. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p11]
> His work made it possible to determine blood group and allowed blood transfusions to be carried out much more safely. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p3]

Cited: alce::-2777825999784077693::p1, alce::-2777825999784077693::p11, alce::-2777825999784077693::p3

### `-673487982339290600`
> France joined the American Revolution on the colonists' side in 1778. [alce::-673487982339290600::p10]
> This was formalized through the Treaty of Alliance in 1778, which established the Franco-American alliance and led France to enter the war against Great Britain. [alce::-673487982339290600::p41]
> France had previously supplied the colonists with covert aid while remaining officially neutral, but this changed with the 1778 alliance. [alce::-673487982339290600::p41]

Cited: alce::-673487982339290600::p10, alce::-673487982339290600::p41

### `1040777011551693796`
> A joint session of the Indian Parliament is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. [alce::1040777011551693796::p1]
> In the absence of the Speaker, it is presided over by the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha, or in his absence, by the Deputy-Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. [alce::1040777011551693796::p0]
> If none of these officers are present, any other member of Parliament can preside by consensus of both Houses. [alce::1040777011551693796::p3]

### `2469112227919120438`
> The creation of the Senate was based on different foundations depending on which Senate is being referenced:

For the United States Senate, it was formed on the example of the ancient Roman Senate. [alce::2469112227919120438::p21]
> Additionally, the U.S. Senate was created out of the refusal of small states to go along with the US Constitution unless they were granted equal suffrage in one body of the national legislature. [alce::2469112227919120438::p0]
> For the Canadian Senate, it was based on the Westminster model (the model of the Parliament of the United Kingdom). [alce::2469112227919120438::p3]
> For the Ceylon Senate, it was modelled on the House of Lords in the United Kingdom. [alce::2469112227919120438::p4]

Cited: alce::2469112227919120438::p0, alce::2469112227919120438::p21, alce::2469112227919120438::p3, alce::2469112227919120438::p4

### `4537952778249650608`
> The latitudes that experience the greatest annual change in daylight hours are the polar regions. [alce::4537952778249650608::p23]
> Within 23.5° latitude of the poles, the seasonal variations in the length of daytime are extreme, with at least some days each year during which the sun never goes below the horizon, and other days when the sun never rises above the horizon. [alce::4537952778249650608::p22]
> Both the poles experience sunlight for 6 months and darkness for 6 months, at opposite times of the year. [alce::4537952778249650608::p0]
> In contrast, at middle latitudes, variations in the length of daytime are moderate, with higher middle latitudes experiencing noticeable but limited seasonal differences, while lower middle latitudes experience even smaller seasonal differences in daylight.

Cited: alce::4537952778249650608::p0, alce::4537952778249650608::p22, alce::4537952778249650608::p23

> The first voyage to circumnavigate the globe was led initially by Ferdinand Magellan, a Portuguese navigator, beginning in 1519. [alce::8307493951745307544::p2]
> After Magellan's death in 1521, Juan Sebastián Elcano, a Spanish navigator, took command and completed the expedition in 1522. [alce::8307493951745307544::p20]
> This expedition, known as the Magellan–Elcano circumnavigation, was the first voyage around the world in human history. [alce::8307493951745307544::p2]

Cited: alce::8307493951745307544::p2, alce::8307493951745307544::p20

### `8020759438851523056`
> The federal student loan program started in 1958 under the National Defense Education Act (NDEA). [alce::8020759438851523056::p14]
> These initial loans were only available to select categories of students, such as those studying toward engineering, science, or education degrees. [alce::8020759438851523056::p14]

### `2483689322545297886`
> The timing of woolly mammoth extinction varied by population. Most populations of woolly mammoths in North America and Eurasia died out around the time of the last glacial retreat. [alce::2483689322545297886::p6]
> The woolly mammoths of eastern Beringia died out about 13,300 years ago. [alce::2483689322545297886::p0]

### `2697046418313369999`
> The Mouth of Truth is a marble mask in Rome, Italy, located against the left wall of the portico of the Santa Maria in Cosmedin church at the Piazza della Bocca della Verità. [alce::2697046418313369999::p2]
> The massive marble mask weighs about 1300 kg and probably depicts the face of the sea god Oceanus. [alce::2697046418313369999::p2]
> According to legend, if a person places their hand inside the mouth and swears falsely, the mouth will close and sever the hand, though there is no attested case of such an event taking place. [alce::2697046418313369999::p16]
> The sculpture is thought to be an ancient drain covering. [alce::2697046418313369999::p16]

### `-4508291966620018192`
> The highest temperatures on Earth are recorded in hot deserts. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p8]
> According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the highest registered air temperature on Earth was in Furnace Creek Ranch, California, located in the Death Valley desert in the United States, on July 10, 1913. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p0]
> However, some weather historians claim this 1913 Death Valley reading is unreliable, and if it were decertified, the highest recorded air temperature would be 54°C, recorded both in Death Valley on June 20, 2013, and in Mitribah, Kuwait on July 21, 2016. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p4]
> For ground surface temperatures (rather than air temperatures), a reading of 93.9°C has been recorded. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p5]

### `-2427005357321258325`
> The Virginia Plan gave more power to the national/federal government and to larger states.

The plan granted the federal government legislative power on all issues, as well as the power to nullify state laws. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p2]
> Additionally, the Virginia Plan granted the United States Senate the power to abrogate any law passed by state governments. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p1]
> The plan also threatened to limit smaller states' power by making both houses of the legislature proportionate to population. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p0]
> This meant that larger states with greater populations would have more representation and thus more power in the legislature.

Cited: alce::-2427005357321258325::p0, alce::-2427005357321258325::p1, alce::-2427005357321258325::p2

> The blood-brain barrier is formed by multiple types of cells working together. Endothelial cells of the capillary wall form the primary structural component. [alce::-3553537514837674455::p1]

> Hugo Münsterberg was a prominent pioneer in eyewitness research. [alce::-7795481670164993090::p0]
> His controversial book "On the Witness Stand" (1908) demonstrated the fallibility of eyewitness accounts, though it met with fierce criticism in legal circles. [alce::-7795481670164993090::p0]
> Additionally, Elizabeth Loftus is one of the leading psychologists in the field of eyewitness testimony, having revolutionized the field with extensive research challenging the credibility of eyewitness testimony in court. [alce::-7795481670164993090::p42]
> Gary L. Wells is also an internationally recognized scholar in eyewitness memory research with extensive research on lineup procedures and the reliability and accuracy of eyewitness identification. [alce::-7795481670164993090::p38]

### `-5824736776762973289`
> In Chinese, "yang" (阳) refers to the "sunny side" or the side that receives more direct sunlight. [alce::-5824736776762973289::p0]
> Specifically, "yang" refers to the "south side of a hill" or the "north bank of a river," as these locations receive more sunlight in the Northern Hemisphere. [alce::-5824736776762973289::p0]
> Additionally, "yang" can be the transliteration of the character 楊 (simplified: 杨), which means a type of poplar tree. [alce::-5824736776762973289::p20]
> It can also represent the character 阳, which means "sun." [alce::-5824736776762973289::p10]

### `-5942352520455511076`
> Adipose tissue can be found in several locations throughout the body. In humans, adipose tissue is located beneath the skin (subcutaneous fat), around internal organs (visceral fat), in bone marrow (yellow bone marrow), intermuscular, and in breast tissue. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p0]
> In infants, brown adipose tissue depots are found in the interscapular region, supraclavicular area, suprarenal region, pericardial area, para-aortic region, and around the pancreas, kidney, and trachea. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p5]
> In adults, brown adipose tissue depots are most often detected in the supraclavicular, paravertebral, mediastinal, para-aortic, and suprarenal regions. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p5]

Cited: alce::-5942352520455511076::p0, alce::-5942352520455511076::p5

### `941064240300059394`
> The timing of the Byzantine Empire's decline is a matter of scholarly debate. [alce::941064240300059394::p6]
> However, several key periods mark significant stages in its decline: the Sack of Constantinople in 1204 initiated a slow collapse. [alce::941064240300059394::p1]
> The collapse of imperial power and authority after 1185 revealed structural inadequacies in the empire's governance. [alce::941064240300059394::p12]
> The Latin Christian Fourth Crusade (AD 1202-04) is considered one of the most prominent acts that contributed to the empire's terminal decline. [alce::941064240300059394::p23]
> Although Byzantine Emperors were restored in 1260, the empire could never fully recover and eventually fell to the Ottomans in 1453. [alce::941064240300059394::p42]

### `4653868131958586810`
> NAT (Network Address Translation) is a method of remapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> The technique was originally used as a shortcut to avoid the need to readdress every host when a network was moved. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> It has become a popular and essential tool in conserving global address space in the face of IPv4 address exhaustion. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> One Internet-routable IP address of a NAT gateway can be used for an entire private network. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> NAT works by hiding an entire IP address space, usually consisting of private IP addresses, behind a single IP address in another, usually public address space. [alce::4653868131958586810::p2]

Cited: alce::4653868131958586810::p0, alce::4653868131958586810::p2

> According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the highest registered air temperature on Earth was recorded in Furnace Creek Ranch, California, located in Death Valley, on July 10, 1913. [alce::2900239413928677063::p4]
> However, the WMO has noted that this temperature may have resulted from a sandstorm that caused superheated surface materials to affect the reading, meaning it may not reflect true air temperature. [alce::2900239413928677063::p4]
> If the 1913 record were to be decertified, the highest recorded air temperature on Earth would be the temperature recorded both in Death Valley on June 20, 2013, and in Mitribah, Kuwait on July 21, 2016. [alce::2900239413928677063::p7]

### `-604099956838475859`
> Benjamin is a popular given name for males derived from Hebrew "Binyāmīn," translating as "Son of my right
> ." [alce::-604099956838475859::p0]
> According to the Hebrew Bible, Benjamin's name originated when Jacob deliberately changed the original name "Benoni" to Benjamin. [alce::-604099956838475859::p2]
> The original name "Benoni" meant "son of my pain," as it alluded to Rachel's death immediately after giving birth to him. [alce::-604099956838475859::p3]
> Jacob preferred to call him Benjamin instead, which can be read in Hebrew as meaning "son of my right
> ." [alce::-604099956838475859::p3]

Cited: alce::-604099956838475859::p0, alce::-604099956838475859::p2, alce::-604099956838475859::p3

### `-7338374818994111335`
> Diffusion occurs in several locations within the excretory system:

In the skin, ammonia is expelled directly by diffusion through the skin. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p13]
> In single-celled organisms like amoeba, contractile vacuoles collect excretory wastes such as ammonia from the intracellular fluid by diffusion. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p9]
> In the kidneys, water diffuses out of cells into the blood stream, from where it is excreted in the form of perspiration, water vapor in breath, or urine. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p45]

Cited: alce::-7338374818994111335::p13, alce::-7338374818994111335::p45, alce::-7338374818994111335::p9

### `-285728796551513770`
> The Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches split in 1054 due to a combination of political and theological factors. [alce::-285728796551513770::p9]
> The split was sparked when Pope Leo IX asserted authority over three of the seats in the Pentarchy—Antioch, Jerusalem, and Alexandria—which had been lost to Islamic expansion but were being reclaimed by Western Roman successors. [alce::-285728796551513770::p31]
> Pope Leo also defended the filioque clause in the Nicene Creed, which became a point of theological dispute. [alce::-285728796551513770::p31]
> More broadly, the fracture was caused more by political events than by slight divergences of creed, including popes siding with the king of the Franks, crowning a rival Roman emperor, and appropriating territories in Italy. [alce::-285728796551513770::p9]
> The schism was also caused by historical and linguistic developments that created theological differences between the Western and Eastern churches. [alce::-285728796551513770::p58]
> In 1054, the Bishop of Rome and the four Greek-speaking patriarchs of the East excommunicated each other, triggering the formal East-West Schism. [alce::-285728796551513770::p17]

Cited: alce::-285728796551513770::p17, alce::-285728796551513770::p31, alce::-285728796551513770::p58, alce::-285728796551513770::p9

### `-7865906780005955500`
> The question asks about the last time a $2 bill was printed, but the sources discuss two different countries' $2 bills with different timelines.

For **Canadian $2 bills**: Printing of the $2 note ceased on February 18, 1996. [alce::-7865906780005955500::p4]
> For **U.S. $2 bills**: The most recent printing mentioned was in November 2013, when the B.E.P. began printing series 2013 $2 bills for the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta; these notes entered circulation in early 2014. [alce::-7865906780005955500::p0]
> Additionally, the sources indicate that $2 bills are still being printed, though not as frequently as other denominations. [alce::-7865906780005955500::p1]

### `-8080892130396390822`
> The saying "man's best friend" has multiple origins and early uses. The first recorded use of a related phrase was by Frederick the Great of Prussia, who referred to one of his Italian Greyhounds as his best friend. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]
> Before Frederick, Voltaire had written about this concept in his "Dictionnaire philosophique" of 1764. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p2]
> In the United States, the earliest citation is traced to a poem printed in "The New-York Literary Journal, Volume 4" in 1821. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]
> The phrase was likely popularized by its use in a poem by Ogden Nash. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1]

Cited: alce::-8080892130396390822::p0, alce::-8080892130396390822::p1, alce::-8080892130396390822::p2

### `-3840313627795945784`
> The answer depends on which country's federal law you are asking about:

**For Canada:** R.B. Bennett's government passed the "Employment and Social Insurance Act" in 1935 to establish a national unemployment scheme. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p0]
> However, this was ruled unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of Canada, and the first Canadian unemployment insurance system was adopted in 1940 after a constitutional amendment. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p20]
> **For the United States:** President Roosevelt signed the Social Security Act on August 14, 1935, which provided benefits to the unemployed. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p19]
> Additionally, the U.S. Congress passed the Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) to authorize collection of federal employer taxes to fund state workforce agencies. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p14]
> **For the United Kingdom:** The Unemployment Insurance Act 1920 expanded provisions for unemployment benefits and set up the dole system that provided 39 weeks of unemployment benefits to practically the entire civilian working population. [alce::-3840313627795945784::p7]

Cited: alce::-3840313627795945784::p0, alce::-3840313627795945784::p14, alce::-3840313627795945784::p19, alce::-3840313627795945784::p20, alce::-3840313627795945784::p7

### `-8300695731604896849`
> The first alternating current electric system was developed by multiple contributors working in the 1880s. Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti established the first alternating current power station in the world in 1887, whose design all others would follow. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p21]
> Ferranti built the world's first practical transformer based alternating current power system at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1886. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p6]
> Additionally, Nikola Tesla filed a range of patents related to AC power systems in 1887 and 1888, including one for a practical two-phase induction motor which Westinghouse licensed for his AC system. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p6]

> The Great Wall of China began construction during multiple periods. Several walls were being built as early as the 7th century BC. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p12]

> Later, fortifications built by various states during the Spring and Autumn (771–476) and Warring States periods (475–221) were connected by the first emperor of China, Qin Shi Huang, to protect his newly founded Qin dynasty (221–206). [alce::-7301225247880986147::p2]

### `305726888559328341`
> Metabolic activity in a cell occurs in multiple locations depending on the specific pathway. Most cellular metabolic activities take place within the cytoplasm. [alce::305726888559328341::p0]
> The majority of metabolic processes and metabolites occur in the cytosol, where major metabolic pathways such as protein biosynthesis, the pentose phosphate pathway, glycolysis, and gluconeogenesis take place. [alce::305726888559328341::p1]
> However, some metabolic pathways occur in other cellular compartments; for instance, the citric acid cycle, electron transport chain, and oxidative phosphorylation all take place in the mitochondrial membrane. [alce::305726888559328341::p9]

Cited: alce::305726888559328341::p0, alce::305726888559328341::p1, alce::305726888559328341::p9

### `7803488668042298223`
> The term "cupboard love" comes from a learning theory of the 1950s and 1960s based on psychoanalytic research. [alce::7803488668042298223::p0]
> The theory, rooted in psychoanalysis, speculates that attachment develops in infancy when the mother satisfies the infant's instinctual needs, and cupboard love theorists conclude that the primary drive during infancy is food, which leads to a secondary drive for attachment. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> Sigmund Freud was the first to suggest that attachment results from the mother fulfilling her infant's physiological needs, and through this process of gratification, the infant begins to desire the mother—a form of attachment often referred to as cupboard love. [alce::7803488668042298223::p4]

Cited: alce::7803488668042298223::p0, alce::7803488668042298223::p2, alce::7803488668042298223::p4

### `6635099307335066088`
> The International Red Cross was established in 1863. [alce::6635099307335066088::p0]
> It was created because Henry Dunant, after witnessing the Battle of Solferino in 1859, was shocked by the lack of facilities, personnel, and medical aid available to help wounded soldiers. [alce::6635099307335066088::p69]
> His wartime experiences inspired Dunant to propose the establishment of the Red Cross in Geneva. [alce::6635099307335066088::p69]
> Specifically, the International Committee for Aid to Wounded in Situations of War was created as a permanent sub-committee in 1863 by the Geneva Society of Public Welfare, and a constitutive conference in October 1863 created the statutory foundation of the International Committee of the Red Cross. [alce::6635099307335066088::p0]

Cited: alce::6635099307335066088::p0, alce::6635099307335066088::p69

> Of the igneous rocks, basalts and gabbros are the most abundant, comprising 66% of igneous rocks in the crust. [alce::228510935925327011::p0]
> More specifically, basalt is the most common volcanic rock type on Earth and makes up the oceanic crust. [alce::228510935925327011::p10]

### `3055530995120092075`
> The Navigation Acts were the primary laws that directed the flow of goods between England and the colonies. [alce::3055530995120092075::p43]
> These laws were first enacted in 1651 under the Commonwealth, and were reenacted and broadened with the restoration by the Act of 1660, then further developed and tightened by the Navigation Acts of 1663, 1673, and 1696. [alce::3055530995120092075::p43]
> The Navigation Acts required that English colonial goods be carried only in English ships and legislated limits on trade between the English colonies and foreigners. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> Additionally, the Staple Act of 1663 was passed to regulate colonial trade. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> These Acts were designed to direct most American trade to England and to encourage the manufacture of goods for export to the colonies in Britain. [alce::3055530995120092075::p90]
> The Acts banned foreign ships from transporting goods from Asia, Africa or America to England or its colonies; only ships with an English owner, master and a majority English crew would be accepted. [alce::3055530995120092075::p49]

Cited: alce::3055530995120092075::p1, alce::3055530995120092075::p43, alce::3055530995120092075::p49, alce::3055530995120092075::p90

### `7986410680084895333`
> India conducted nuclear tests at Pokhran on two separate occasions:

First, India conducted its first nuclear test at Pokhran on May 18, 1974. [alce::7986410680084895333::p56]
> This test was code-named "Smiling Buddha" and was termed a "peaceful nuclear explosion" by India. [alce::7986410680084895333::p56]
> Second, India conducted a series of five nuclear tests at Pokhran in May 1998. [alce::7986410680084895333::p1]
> These tests, known as Pokhran-II and assigned the code name "Operation Shakti," were initiated on 11 May 1998. [alce::7986410680084895333::p1]

Cited: alce::7986410680084895333::p1, alce::7986410680084895333::p56

### `8664313149431483137`
> The human musculoskeletal system is an organ system that gives humans the ability to move using their muscular and skeletal systems. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> It is made up of the bones of the skeleton, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, joints, and other connective tissue that supports and binds tissues and organs together. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system performs several key functions. It provides form, support, stability, and movement to the body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> Its primary functions include supporting the body, allowing motion, and protecting vital organs. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> It gives the body basic structure and the ability for movement. [alce::8664313149431483137::p15]

### `5398357621914791303`
> The practice of baptism has multiple origins depending on the tradition:

The Christian ritual of baptism traces back to the baptism of Jesus by John the Baptist, who baptized Jesus in the Jordan River. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]
> John the Baptist adopted baptism as the central sacrament in his messianic movement, seen as a forerunner of Christianity. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]
> More broadly, the practice of purification via immersion exists in many cultures. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]
> A practice similar to baptism is known to have been practiced by the Jewish Essenes between the 2nd century BC and the 1st century AD. [alce::5398357621914791303::p16]
> The Greek noun "baptmos" was used to refer to ritual washing in Hellenistic Judaism. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]
> Christians consider Jesus to have instituted the sacrament of baptism. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]
> Baptism has been part of Christianity from the start, as shown by the many mentions in the Acts of the Apostles and the Pauline epistles. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]

Cited: alce::5398357621914791303::p1, alce::5398357621914791303::p16, alce::5398357621914791303::p4
